title: U.S. Web Design System (USWDS) for United States Government
content: # U.S. Web Systems Directories Components Articles Blueprints Guides Glossary Biweekly Digest Created by Adobe Alaska Airlines Amazon Apple Atlassian Audi Basis Biings Brainly Carrefour Cash App CBRE Build Cisco Datadog Dell Docplanner eBay Elastic Esri General Electric Github GitLab Google GOV.UK Gusto Helly Hansen IBM Ideagen INPS Kajabi Kiwicom Lemon Squeezy Line LocalTapiola Michelin Microsoft Monday.com MongoDB Motorway Mozilla Nordhealth Ontario Orange Palantir Pinterest Pluralsight Porsche Razorpay Red Hat Rei Salesforce Samsung Seek Segment ServiceNow Shopify Skoda Sprout Social SumUp Sunrise Teamleader Thumbtack Twilio UAE Uber Vercel Visa Washington Post Wikimedia Wise Wonderflow Workday WorkOS Zendesk Keep up with the latest in Design Systems Subscribe Design System from Directories: Components: The U.S. Web Design System (USWDS) is the federal government's answer to making digital life easier for everyday Americans. This system gives government web developers the tools they need—everything from color schemes to button designs—to build sites that actually work for real people. The goal is simple: make government websites consistent, accessible, and efficient. Hundreds of teams across all levels of government use it, improving it based on how people actually interact with their sites. It's a rare example of government tech that evolves through genuine public collaboration. est in Design Systems Subscribe Design System from Directories: Components: The U.S. Web Design System (USWDS) is the federal government's answer to making digital life easier for everyday Americans. This system gives government web developers the tools they need—everything from color schemes to button designs—to build sites that actually work for real people. The goal is simple: make government websites consistent, accessible, and efficient. Hundreds of teams across all levels of government use it, improving it based on how people actually interact with their sites. It's a rare example of government tech that evolves through genuine public collaboration.
